actions.thought: With King-Deuce offsuit from the Button in a 3-handed game, the hand is too weak to open-raise or limp. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve our stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with T8o. The button folded, making this a heads-up pot against the big blind. Completing the blind is a standard strategy here to see a flop cheaply with decent implied odds, rather than folding and forfeiting the 50 chips already committed. Given the early stage of the tournament, taking a low-variance line to see the flop is appropriate.

I have a solid suited connector (78s) in the Big Blind. The Small Blind limped in, and I have the option to see a free flop. While raising is an option to exert pressure, checking allows me to realize the equity of this hand without bloating the pot out of position or inducing a reraise. I will take the free flop.

I have middle pair with a Ten on a rainbow board (7d Qh Ts). The big blind checked to me, which often signals a range capped at marginal hands or air. By betting, I can gain value from weaker hands, protect against potential overcards, and take control of the hand early. A bet of 100 (half the pot) is an appropriate size to build the pot without overcommitting on a board where my kicker is relatively weak.

I have bottom pair (7s) on a board of Qh-Ts-7d. My opponent limped from the Small Blind and has now bet 100 into a 200 pot. Given the pot odds (3:1 on the call) and the fact that my opponent's limping range can include many draws (like J9 or 98) or weaker hands that I am currently beating, folding would be too tight. I have 5 outs to improve to two pair or three-of-a-kind. Raising would likely only get called by better hands, so I will call to see how the turn develops.

The board has become significantly more dangerous with the addition of the 6d on the turn, which completes potential straight draws like 8-9 and introduces a flush draw. My hand, a pair of Tens with an 8 kicker, has lost significant relative value. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that have me beat or by draws that have high equity against me. Checking is the most prudent action to maintain pot control and realize my equity to the river without overcommitting to a bloated pot with a mediocre pair.

I have bottom pair (sevens) and a gutshot straight draw (any nine). Flashlight led the flop but has now checked the turn. This check often indicates a marginal hand like a Ten or a missed draw, or perhaps a cautious Queen. Betting here might force out some air, but it's unlikely to fold out a Ten or a Queen, and I don't want to get check-raised off my equity. Checking back allows me to see a free river, potentially hitting a straight or two pair, and keeps the pot manageable for my marginal showdown value.

The river King of Diamonds is a very dangerous card, completing potential flushes and straights while also giving top pair to any King in the opponent's range. My pair of Tens is effectively a bluff-catcher now. Given that I checked the turn, checking the river is the most logical move to reach a showdown at the lowest cost. I cannot gain value by betting, as only better hands would call, and I need to be prepared to fold if my opponent makes a significant bet.

The river King of Diamonds is a scare card that completes a potential flush and some straights (though AJ and J9 are the primary ones). Flashlight's sequence of leading the flop and then checking both the turn and river suggests a marginal hand like a Ten or a weak Queen that is now playing cautiously, or a missed draw. My pair of sevens has very little showdown value, but betting here is risky as it is unlikely to fold out a Queen or a Ten in a small pot. Since I have some showdown value against complete air or missed lower draws, and I am unlikely to benefit from turning my hand into a bluff against this specific range, checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown for free.

Jack-four offsuit is a very weak hand with no playability or potential for strong combinations. Given the current pot odds and the weak strength of my holding, folding is the only strategically sound decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Even though it is only 50 more to call, this hand plays poorly post-flop and I would be out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

62o is an extremely weak hand with no playability or high-card strength. As the button in a three-handed game, while I could potentially bluff, this hand is far too weak to justify any investment. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

With J7 suited in the small blind and the action folded to me, I have a decent hand to attack the big blind. Raising takes the initiative and puts pressure on the big blind's wide defending range. While limping is an option, a raise to 300 can often take down the pot right here or give me the betting lead going into the flop.

Queen-Seven offsuit is a weak holding that plays poorly out of position, especially against an opening raise from the small blind. Given the early stage of the tournament and the deep stack, there is no need to play a marginal hand out of position; folding is the disciplined and correct strategic choice.

K6s is a decently playable hand on the button in a 3-handed game. A min-raise to 200 is a standard steal attempt that often picks up the blinds or gives us position post-flop with suited flush potential and top pair equity.

Jack-three offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability and equity. Being in the small blind puts me at a severe positional disadvantage for the rest of the hand. Defending the big blind or calling from the small blind with such a marginal hand against a button open is mathematically incorrect and leads to significant chip leakage. Folding is the disciplined, optimal play.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6c5c, a solid suited connector. The Button (Pro) has raised to 200. With the Small Blind folding, I need to call 100 more chips to see the flop in a pot that will be 450. I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds, which is more than enough to defend with this hand. Calling allows me to realize my equity and play a pot in position against a likely wide button opening range without bloating the pot pre-flop with a speculative hand.

I have 6-high on an Ace-high board with two diamonds. As the Big Blind who defended against a button raise, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor. My hand has very little equity or draw potential on this texture, so checking is the standard and most prudent play.

The board of 8d Td As hits the Big Blind caller's range decently well (flush draws, straight draws, pair of Tens or Eights). We have K6 of hearts, giving us no backdoor flush draw and no real equity besides a weak King if we hit it. Given our lack of equity and the wetness of the board, checking back to see a free card is the most prudent play. C-betting here with pure air is likely to get called too often by draws and pairs.

I have 6-high with no draws on a very coordinated board (8d-Td-As-Qc). The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked back on the flop, which often indicates some showdown value or a hand that gave up. However, this board hits a Button opening range very well. Since I have zero equity and no realistic way to win the hand without a significant bluff, and considering I am out of position, checking is the most sensible play. I will likely fold to any bet.

We have King-high with a gutshot to the nut straight (any Jack gives us A-K-Q-J-T). Flash has checked twice, which could indicate a weak pair, a draw, or giving up. While a delayed c-bet could fold out some weaker hands, taking a free card to realize our straight equity is a low-risk, solid play. Betting here is likely to get called by Ax, Qx, or Tx, against which we have poor equity.

I have 6-high with no showdown value on a board of 8d-Td-As-Qc-7c. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has checked both the flop and the turn, which suggests they may have a marginal hand with some showdown value or a missed draw. While a bluff could potentially take down the 450-chip pot, the board is highly coordinated and hits many parts of a standard Button opening range. Given it is early in the tournament and my hand has zero equity, I will check and likely concede the pot rather than attempting a low-probability bluff into a range that likely contains better high cards or small pairs.

With King-high and no pair on a board of 8-T-A-Q-7, I have essentially no showdown value, but my opponent has checked down all three streets, indicating a weak holding or a missed draw (such as missed diamonds or a straight draw). Since I checked the flop and turn, a sudden river bet looks suspiciously like a bluff and is highly likely to be called by any weak pair (8x, Tx, or Qx) that my opponent decided to check down. Taking the safe route and checking back to see if King-high is miraculously good against a missed draw is the best play to preserve chips.

I am on the button with 8d5d, which is a decent suited connector for opening from this position. Stealing the blinds is a key part of maintaining a healthy stack, and I have positional advantage if called. A raise to 2.5x (250) is a standard opening size to put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 3s. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 250. While the hand is suited, it is extremely weak (a 9-high gapper) and I would be playing out of position against both the Button and the Big Blind if I call. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x open from the Button. My hand, 7h 9c, is an offsuit gapper and too weak to defend even against a late-position steal attempt. Folding is the optimal play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. In a three-handed game, while the button range should be wide, J4o is significantly below the standard opening range. Given the deep stacks (95 BBs) and the lack of specific reads on the blinds' folding tendencies, there is no reason to play this marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
With Q2s in the small blind, the hand is generally too weak to play, even in a blind vs. blind scenario. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve chips out of position.
K4o is a very weak hand to open from the button/UTG in a 3-handed game. I don't have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or call, so folding is the optimal and standard play here.

With Ace-Five offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. I have a slight equity advantage and the ability to steal the pot pre-flop. Raising to 300 is a standard move to apply pressure and maintain the initiative, rather than just calling and playing out of position post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The Small Blind has raised to 300, which is a 3x open. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, Q4o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. It is early in the tournament and there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand against a standard open. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here.

I am holding 6c and Ts in the button position. This is a very weak hand with no connection potential and poor high-card value. In a 3-handed game, while position is valuable, playing trash hands against blinds is a leak. It is much better to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 8h, a weak offsuit hand. The button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro) and me. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, 58o plays poorly out of position and is easily dominated by Pro's range. Given the early stage of the tournament and my position, folding is the most prudent action to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with A6o in a three-handed game. Ace-high is a strong starting hand in this configuration, and raising from the button is a standard play to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot with positional advantage. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is a standard sizing to accomplish this.
Ten-Eight offsuit is a weak hand, especially out of position in the small blind facing a 3x open from the button. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to avoid bleeding chips.

KJo is a strong hand for defending the big blind against a button open. With 30.7% pot odds, calling is mathematically sound and allows me to see a flop with a decent range advantage. Given the stack depths, I prefer to keep the pot manageable rather than 3-betting, as I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand.

I hold top pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board of Jc-9s-5h. Being out of position as the pre-flop caller, the standard play is to check to the original aggressor (Flash on the button). This allows me to gauge their aggression and pot size, keeping the pot manageable while I have a solid, though not invincible, hand. Betting out here would likely only be called by better hands or cause folds from worse hands I might want to keep in, whereas checking allows me to check-call effectively.

As the pre-flop aggressor on the button, I have a range advantage on this board. Although I only have Ace-high, a small continuation bet (approx. 1/3 pot) is a standard play to put pressure on the Big Blind's defending range and potentially end the hand immediately. If called, I still have overcard equity to an Ace and can re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold top pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry, rainbow board. Flash's bet of 225 into an 875 pot is a small sizing, offering me excellent pot odds (around 4:1) to continue. Given the position and stack depth, calling is the most balanced strategy. It keeps Flash's potential bluffs in the hand and avoids inflating the pot unnecessarily against a range that might contain sets or better top pairs like AJ or KJ suited.

With both players having checked through the turn, the pot size is 1100 and the board is Jc-9s-5h-4h. My top pair of Jacks with a King kicker remains a strong hand that should have significant showdown value. Given the lack of action on the turn, betting here is unlikely to induce value from weaker hands and would only serve to bloat the pot if I am against a better hand or a draw. Checking is the most prudent path to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible while waiting to see the river card.

Flashlight called my flop continuation bet, which suggests they have a piece of this board, likely a pair of 9s, 5s, or possibly a Jack. The 4h on the turn doesn't change much. Since I only have Ace-high and Flashlight hasn't shown weakness yet, checking behind is the most prudent play. It allows me to see a free river and potentially win if they were on a draw or if I improve to a pair of Aces.

The river card (4s) does not significantly change the board texture, and my pair of Jacks remains a solid holding. Given that my opponent checked behind on the turn, it is unlikely they are trapping with a monster hand. Betting now would likely only be called by better hands or cause folds from worse hands I might potentially beat at showdown. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown and minimize risk.

I have Ace-high on a board of J-9-5-4-4. My opponent, Flashlight, has checked to me on the river after calling a flop continuation bet and checking the turn. While Ace-high is weak, it does have some showdown value in a three-handed game against missed draws like 87 or 76. Flashlight's line suggests a marginal hand like a 9 or a 5, or possibly a missed draw. Since my turn check signaled weakness, a bluff on the river is unlikely to fold out a pair. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free and potentially win against non-paired hands.

I am on the button with Q2s. While being on the button in a 3-handed game allows for a wide opening range, Q2s is still a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, often being dominated by better queens. The safest and most standard play here is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Seven offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against the big blind. Given that it is early in the session and there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a mediocre hand, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack.

4s 8s is a weak hand that does not warrant opening or calling a standard big blind in this position. Folding is the most disciplined play to maintain stack efficiency in the early stages of the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 7h, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The button has folded, leaving only myself and the player in the Big Blind. While it would only cost 50 chips to call and see a flop, this hand has extremely poor playability and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against a 'Pro' player. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the hand's lack of connectivity, suitedness, and high-card value, it is not worth a raise or a call in this position, even at a three-handed table. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
With a low pocket pair like 33 in the Small Blind, limping is a solid strategy. It allows us to see a cheap flop and try to set-mine without bloating the pot out of position against the Big Blind.

I am in the big blind with 3h 5h. The small blind has completed the bet, making the pot 200 chips. Given that my hand is weak and there is no raise, checking is the standard play to see the flop without committing any additional chips.

I limped pre-flop with a low pocket pair hoping to hit a set. The flop comes with a King and a Jack, along with a flush draw. My pocket threes are heavily out-flopped and have almost no showdown value or equity against any calling range. I will check here with the intention of folding to any significant bet.

I hold 3h 5h on a board of Js Kd 4s. I have no pair, no draw, and zero equity. Since the small blind checked to me, checking is the only logical play to see the turn for free without wasting chips on a bluff that is unlikely to succeed.

The turn brings an Ace, making the board even worse for my pocket threes. The board is now very coordinated and heavily favors the broadway cards that my opponent could hold. Since my hand has zero showdown value and no draws, checking is the only logical play. I will fold if facing a bet.

My hand, 3h 5h, has zero equity on a board of Js Kd 4s Ac. There are no pairs, straight draws, or flush draws. Since the opponent checked the turn, the most logical decision is to check behind to avoid losing more chips with a hopeless hand. A bluff is unlikely to succeed on this high-card heavy board as it interacts well with the opponent's range.

We reached the river with an underpair to a very high board (J-K-4-A-7). Our pocket threes have almost no showdown value, beating only missed draws or exactly pocket twos. Since our opponent checked down the whole way, they might have a weak one-pair hand or a missed draw. Trying to bluff now in a small pot isn't worth the risk, so the best play is to check and hope to get to showdown cheaply or fold to a bet.

The board is Js Kd 4s Ac 7h. I hold 3h 5h, which is 5-high and has no showdown value. Since my opponent, Pro, checked the river, the best course of action is to check behind. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a range that likely includes at least an Ace, King, or Jack, and I have zero equity. Therefore, checking to see the result is the standard play.

Being on the button in a 3-handed game, King-Three suited is a reasonable hand to open-raise with. I want to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot right now, or at worst, play a heads-up pot in position.

I hold pocket sevens, which is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. By 3-betting from the small blind, I can isolate the button (Pro), who opened the betting, and prevent the big blind (Flash) from seeing a cheap flop. This allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or define the range of my opponent. A raise to 650 is an appropriate 3-bet sizing given the open to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with JTo. The Button opened for a min-raise, and the Small Blind 3-bet to 650. JTo is a hand that is easily dominated by a standard 3-betting range (like AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, or medium-to-high pairs). Calling 550 more would mean playing a bloated pot out of position against potentially two opponents or at least the 3-bettor. This is a clear fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

While King-Three suited is an acceptable hand to open from the Button to steal the blinds, it does not play well against a 3-bet. Flashlight's re-raise to 650 from the Small Blind indicates a strong range that heavily dominates K3s. Despite having positional advantage, calling here would inflate the pot with a marginal holding that is easily dominated by better Kings or stronger hands. The optimal decision is to fold and preserve the stack.

Queen-four offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game on the button. There is no tactical advantage to playing this hand, so folding is the correct professional decision to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I have a pocket pair (4s 4c) in the Small Blind. After the Button folds, it's just me and the Big Blind. Pocket pairs are strong in short-handed play. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or set up a play post-flop. Raising is more effective than just completing the blind to put pressure on the Big Blind and define their range.

I am in the Big Blind with J2o, a very weak hand. The Small Blind raised to 300. Calling or raising with J2o from the Big Blind is generally a losing play due to the lack of equity and playability post-flop. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

In a 3-handed game, King-Trey offsuit (K3o) on the button is a marginal hand. While it's tempting to steal the blinds, both Pro and Flashlight have shown they are willing to defend or 3-bet. K3o has very poor playability post-flop and is easily dominated by any other King or Ace in the blinds' ranges. I'll choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position, as I am currently the shortest stack and don't want to bleed chips on sub-par holdings.

Holding J5o in the small blind after the button folds is a very weak starting hand. Stealing against the big blind with this holding is not profitable in the long run, so the standard play is to fold.

With QTo on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a clear opening hand. Raising allows me to steal the blinds or play post-flop in position with a decently connected hand. I'll open to 2.5x the big blind.

I am in the small blind with Jd7d, which is a playable hand against a button open. Given the stack depths and the fact that I will be out of position post-flop, calling is the most balanced approach to realize equity while keeping the pot manageable. I am getting good pot odds to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qd Js. The Button opened for 250 and the Small Blind flat-called. I need to put in 150 more to call. With pot odds of 4:1 and a hand as strong as QJo in a 3-handed dynamic, this is a standard defend. I will call to see the flop.

The board comes down Ace-high, which is very difficult for my hand to connect with. Given I am out of position in a three-way pot and hold only Jack-high with a backdoor flush draw, checking is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack and see what the pre-flop aggressor decides to do. Betting here would be an unnecessary bluff against two opponents.

On an Ace-high board like As-Tc-6d, the pre-flop raiser (Pro) has a significant range advantage. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a King). Flashlight has already checked. Leading into the aggressor here rarely makes sense given the board texture. Checking is the most standard and disciplined play to see how the pre-flop raiser reacts.

With middle pair and a Queen kicker on an Ace-high flop, betting here mostly gets called by better hands like an Ace, while folding out worse hands. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and keep the opponents' ranges wider for future streets.

I am in the small blind with Jack-high and no pair. The board (As-Tc-6d-6h) is very static and likely hit the pre-flop aggressor or the big blind who defended. With three players in the pot and no meaningful equity, checking is the only logical play to control the pot size and avoid throwing away chips on a board that I have completely missed.

The turn has paired the Six, which is unlikely to have improved either opponent's hand given the pre-flop action. The flop was checked around, suggesting that Pro (the pre-flop aggressor) may not have a strong Ace. Flashlight has checked again. I still hold a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (King). While a bet might take the pot down, checking back is a lower-variance play that allows me to realize my equity and see the river for free. If a King arrives, I will likely have the best hand.

With the turn bringing a second six, my pair of Tens is still likely good given the double checks from both opponents. However, betting here is thin for value; worse hands like low pocket pairs or high-card floats will mostly fold, while better hands like a trapping Ace or an unlikely six will call or raise. Checking behind again maintains pot control, avoids getting check-raised off my equity, and allows me to safely navigate to the river.

I am in the small blind with Jack-high, having completely missed the board of A-T-6-6-Q. There is zero showdown value in my hand, and betting into two opponents after it was checked around twice is highly unprofitable. Checking is the standard play to minimize losses in this spot.

The board is As-Tc-6d-6h-Qh. I have a pair of Queens with a Jack kicker. The pre-flop raiser (Pro) checked both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests they do not have an Ace. Flashlight has checked every street, indicating a weak range. Since the river brought me second pair (Queens), I likely have the best hand now. However, betting might only get called by better hands (like an Ace that was slow-played, or QK) or a Ten. By checking, I can safely get to showdown and win the pot against missed draws or smaller pairs without risking a check-raise or value-owning myself.

With Queens and Tens on an Ace-high board, my two pair is effectively beaten by any opponent holding an Ace, as Aces and Sixes would beat my Queens and Tens. Since both opponents checked down all three streets, they likely have marginal showdown value like a Ten, a weak Queen, or a weak Ace. Betting here is too thin for value because better hands like an Ace, trips (6x), or a straight (KJ) will call, while worse hands are likely to fold. Checking behind guarantees a free showdown.

6-5 offsuit is a very weak hand in a three-handed game. As the button, I want to play hands with more equity or potential to win the pot post-flop. With such a marginal starting hand, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In this blind-vs-blind scenario, I am in the Small Blind with 92s. While suited hands have some value, 92s is near the bottom of the range and realizes equity poorly out of position. Against the Big Blind (Pro), who is likely to defend or 3-bet aggressively, folding is a disciplined and standard play to avoid being put in difficult spots with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of deuces. In a three-handed game, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand, especially from the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the blinds, while also giving myself the opportunity to set-mine if called.

We hold Ace-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind facing a Button open to 3bb from Flash. AQo is a premium hand that is well ahead of a typical Button raising range. I will 3-bet to take the initiative, build the pot for value, and try to isolate or win the pot immediately pre-flop. A 3-bet size of around 1000 is standard from out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, 3h-2c. Facing a raise and a 3-bet from my opponents, there is no strategic value in continuing. Folding is the only reasonable decision here as my hand lacks equity and the price to call is significant.

I opened with a standard raise from the button with pocket deuces. Pro has responded with a significant 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. While 22 is a made hand, it is the weakest possible pair and is highly vulnerable to any overcards on the board. For set-mining to be profitable, I generally look for implied odds of at least 15:1. With a call of 700 and effective stacks around 8200, the ratio is only about 11.7:1. Given the large size of the 3-bet and the fact that I will likely have to fold on almost any flop where I don't hit a set, the most disciplined play here is to fold and wait for a better spot, rather than calling off nearly 10% of my stack in a marginal situation.

Holding 95o on the Button, this is a very weak and unsuited hand with practically no post-flop playability. Attempting to steal the blinds or play this hand is negative expected value, so the correct decision is to simply fold.

With the button having folded, I am in the small blind with Ten-Eight suited. This is a reasonable hand to play heads-up against the big blind, especially given the small cost to complete the bet (50 chips). I will call to see a flop in position against the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Q5o. Flashlight (Small Blind) has just completed the blind. Q5o is a weak hand, but I have the option to see a flop for free by checking. There is no reason to raise here as Q5o does not play well against a calling range, and I'd rather keep the pot small with a marginal hand.

I have middle pair with an eight kicker on a board that contains a Jack, a Ten, and two diamonds. Since I am in the small blind and acted first, checking is the most prudent move to control the size of the pot and evaluate how the big blind responds. Betting here could lead to bloating the pot with a marginal hand that might be behind, especially if the big blind has any high-card hands or better kicker cards.

I have bottom pair (Fives) on a somewhat coordinated board (Td-5d-Jc). Flashlight checked from the small blind. In a limped pot, my hand has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value, as I'm likely to only be called by better hands or draws. Checking back allows me to keep the pot small and see a turn card for free, maintaining pot control with a marginal hand.

I hold a pair of Tens on a board of Td-5d-Jc-4c. My opponent (Flash) has checked both the flop and the turn, signaling weakness. Since my pair of Tens is likely the best hand currently but is vulnerable to overcards or draws, I will place a small value bet of 100 into the 200-chip pot to either take the pot now or extract value from weaker hands like a lower pair or an Ace-high that is hesitant to fold.

I have bottom pair (Fives) on a board of Td-5d-Jc-4c. In a limped pot, Flashlight's lead of 100 into 200 on the turn is a small 'probe' bet. Given that Flashlight checked the flop and only bet once the turn bricked, my pair of fives has significant showdown value as a bluff catcher. I am getting 3:1 odds to call, which is more than enough to justify continuing against a wide range that includes draws (diamonds, clubs, or gutshots like Q9/98) and weaker pairs. Raising would likely only isolate me against better hands, so calling to keep the pot manageable is the best play.

I hold two pair (Tens and Eights) on a board of Td-5d-Jc-4c-8h. While this is a strong hand, it is vulnerable to straights (such as 6-7 or 9-7) and sets. Given my opponent (Flash) called a turn bet, they likely have some showdown value or a draw. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and reach showdown without risking chips against a superior hand.

On the river, the board is Td-5d-Jc-4c-8h. I have bottom pair (Fives). Flashlight checked after betting the turn. In a limped pot, Flashlight's turn bet followed by a river check often indicates a marginal hand or a missed draw. My pair of fives has significant showdown value here against missed diamond draws or straight draws like Q9 or 97. I am in the Big Blind holding 3c 2s, which is arguably the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Pro raised from the Button and Flashlight called from the Small Blind. Although I only need to put in 150 more to see a flop in a pot that would be 750, this hand has almost no playability and very poor equity even in a multi-way pot. I am currently the shortest stack at the table, and while I am not in immediate danger, wasting chips on trash hands out of position is a recipe for a slow exit. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have two gutshot straight draws (any 3 or 7 completes a straight) on a low board of 8-4-2. This is a solid semi-bluffing board that hits my range as the small blind caller better than the button's typical pre-flop raising range. Since I am out of position, checking to the original raiser is the standard approach to control the pot size and evaluate their strength before deciding whether to call a bet or potentially check-raise.

On this dry 8-high board, I have a backdoor flush draw, a backdoor straight draw, and two overcards to the middle of the board. My opponent likely missed this board a significant portion of the time with their pre-flop calling range, which consists of broadway cards and suited connectors. A standard continuation bet of 1/3 pot should be enough to fold out better high-card hands like King-high or Queen-high.

The pot is 800 and the opponent has bet 200, giving me 4:1 pot odds. My hand, 5c6c, on a board of 8s-4d-2h, holds a gutshot straight draw (needs a 3 or 7 to complete the straight), giving me 4 outs. While the direct odds are slightly against me, the price is attractive enough to warrant a call to see if I can improve on the turn, especially since I can continue to realize my equity in the hand.

With the turn card 7d, I have now completed an open-ended straight draw (5-6-7-8), giving me 8 outs to a straight (any 3 or 9). Being out of position, checking is the most standard and disciplined approach to control the pot size while I continue to realize my equity. This allows me to evaluate the opponent's next move before committing more chips to a draw.

The turn brings a 7, giving me middle pair. My hand now has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to value bet against the hands that called the flop (like an 8, better pocket pairs, or strong draws). Betting risks getting check-raised and forces me to fold a hand with decent equity. Checking behind exercises pot control, allows me to realize my equity safely, and keeps my opponent's missed floats or draws in their range for the river.

I have completed an 8-high straight (4-5-6-7-8) with my 5c6c on the river board of 8s-4d-2h-7d-6s. This is a very strong hand. Since the action was checked through on the turn and the opponent checked on the river, it is highly likely that the opponent has a marginal hand (such as a pair or a missed draw) that they would be willing to call with. I am betting 500, which is half the pot, to extract value from these hands while still being a size that invites a call.

On this board, my pair of sevens is only third pair. When Flashlight leads for half-pot on the river after the turn checked through, their range heavily consists of value hands like an 8, a rivered two pair, or a straight (any 5 or 10-9). While they might occasionally be bluffing with missed backdoor draws or random broadways, my hand is too weak of a bluff-catcher to call profitably. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.
AQ offsuit is a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I should take initiative by raising to 300 to build a pot and potentially isolate an opponent or pick up the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 5d, which is a very weak starting hand. Flashlight has raised from the Button, and Pro is still to act in the Big Blind. Given my hand's poor connectivity and the fact that I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no reason to defend. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.
K8o is a weak offsuit hand, and facing a 3x raise from the Button while out of position, it plays very poorly post-flop. The best decision is to fold and look for a better spot.

I have a pocket pair of Sixes on the button in a three-handed game. This is a strong hand that should be played aggressively pre-flop to either take the blinds or build a pot where I likely have the best hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300.
65o is too weak of a hand to play from the small blind against a button raise, especially out of position and unsuited. The best decision is to fold and save chips for a better spot.

Holding Ace-Five offsuit in the big blind against a button open, I have sufficient pot odds to defend. With a 3x open raise, calling 200 to win a pot of 450 is mathematically sound. A5o has reasonable high-card equity and allows me to see a flop and evaluate the texture while closing the action pre-flop.

I am out of position in the big blind with Ace-high on a board of 9h-8s-2h. I have no pair and very little equity, so checking is the standard play to see if the button checks back or if I can potentially realize equity or fold to heavy pressure.

I have pocket sixes on a 9-8-2 rainbow board with a heart draw possible. Flashlight checked from the big blind after calling my pre-flop raise. While there are two overcards to my pair, the board is relatively dry for a big blind calling range, although it hits some suited connectors. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot to protect my hand from overcards and to see where I stand. A bet of 325 is appropriate to maintain initiative.

I hold Ace-Five offsuit on a board of 9h-8s-2h. I have no pair and no meaningful draw other than backdoor potential, which is insufficient to continue against a continuation bet on this board texture. The board hits the button's raising range quite well, and calling a continuation bet with only Ace-high is speculative and likely unprofitable. Given the stack depths and the board texture, folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my chip stack.

With A5o on the button, I have a decent hand to attempt a blind steal. The Ace serves as a good blocker, and I'll have a positional advantage post-flop if called. I'll open with a standard raise to 250 to pressure the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against the Button's opening raise. With the Big Blind still to act and my position being disadvantaged for the remainder of the hand, folding is the most disciplined and prudent strategic choice to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Four offsuit. The button (Pro) raised to 250. To call the remaining 150, I am getting very good pot odds (2.67:1). In three-handed play, any Ace is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a button open. While I could consider a three-bet, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-committing to a marginal hand out of position.

I defended my big blind with Ace-Four offsuit against a button raise from Pro. The flop comes King-high (3d-8c-Ks), which is a board texture that heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Holding only Ace-high, I have some marginal showdown value but very little to gain by leading into the raiser. Checking is the standard and most prudent play here to see how Pro responds. If Pro checks back, I might have the best hand; if Pro bets, I can evaluate the price to see if it's worth continuing with my overcard and backdoors.

The board is K-high, dry, and disconnected, which strongly favors my pre-flop raising range. Since Flash checked, a standard continuation bet of around 1/3 pot is very likely to fold out any of his missed broadways, underpairs, or random floats. It's a profitable spot to c-bet and try to take the pot down right here.

On this dry King-high board (3d-8c-Ks), Pro has followed up their pre-flop raise with a small continuation bet of 200 into a 550 pot. With my Ace-high, I still hold some showdown value in a three-handed game, and the price I am being offered (3.75:1) is very attractive. While my kicker is weak, folding to such a small bet would be overly exploitable. I will call to see a turn and evaluate based on further action or improvement.

I have improved to bottom pair on the turn with my Ace of Spades and Four of Clubs on a board of 3d-8c-Ks-4d. While I now have a pair, it is still a marginal hand on a King-high board. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor remains the most sensible play to control the size of the pot and attempt to reach showdown cheaply. I have some showdown value against missed draws or lower pocket pairs, and checking allows me to evaluate Pro's reaction to the turn card.

The turn brings the 4d, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the wheel. Flash check-called my continuation bet on the flop, indicating they likely connected with the board in some way (a pair of 8s, a weak King, or a middle pocket pair). While barreling could fold out some of their weaker pairs, checking behind controls the pot size and allows me to see a free river card to potentially hit my straight or an Ace.

On the river, the board is 3d-8c-Ks-4d-Jc. I have a pair of fours (bottom pair). After the turn went check-check, it is unlikely the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has a very strong hand like a top-tier King or better, as they would typically bet the turn for value or protection. However, my bottom pair is still quite weak and lacks the strength to bet for value. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value against missed draws and Ace-high hands. If Pro bets, I will have to evaluate the size of the bet, but checking is the best play to reach showdown cheaply.

I missed my gutshot straight draw and am left with Ace high. Flash's check-call on the flop and subsequent checks on the turn and river indicate he likely holds a marginal made hand like a weak pair (8x, 4x, 3x) or a missed draw. While a river bluff might fold out some bottom pairs, the Jack on the river improves some of his flop floats (like JT or QJ). Checking behind allows me to safely realize my showdown value against his missed draws without risking more chips on a bluff that might get called by a stubborn pair.

I am in the button position with King-Six offsuit, which is a weak hand in 3-max play. While the button has position, K-6o lacks the strength to call or raise against the blinds. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

In this three-handed situation, the button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving it to the blinds. I am in the small blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. While it is tempting to complete the blind or raise to steal the pot, Ten-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against an active and competent opponent like 'Pro' in the big blind. Given my current stack size relative to the other players, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a more advantageous position or a stronger hand to engage with, rather than risking chips in a potentially difficult post-flop scenario where I will be out of position.

I am on the button with 4d 6c. This is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button offers a positional advantage post-flop, the raw equity of 4-6 offsuit against two active opponents like Pro and Flashlight is too low to justify a raise. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack.

Holding King-Five offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button folds. This is a marginal holding out of position against the Big Blind, who is also the current chip leader. Folding is a very safe and standard play to avoid inflating the pot out of position with a weak kicker, preserving my stack for better spots.
With Q7o on the button in a 3-handed game, the hand is too weak to justify an opening raise. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve the stack.
Completing the small blind with Queen-Six offsuit to see a flop, keeping the pot small with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h 6s. The Small Blind has just completed the blind (limped). Since there is no further cost to see the flop, I will check to see if I can improve my hand without committing any additional chips. Raising with such a weak hand into the chip leader is unnecessary.

The board is low and disconnected (4c 2d 9s). My hand (Qd 6d) is purely air with only two overcards. Betting into the opponent would be a bluff without much equity or fold equity. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and potentially see a turn card for free.

I have bottom pair (deuces) on a very dry board (9-4-2) in a limped pot. Flashlight checked to me. While I might have the best hand, my kicker (6) is weak and betting doesn't accomplish much against better hands that won't fold, nor does it get much value from worse hands. Checking back allows me to control the pot and see the turn for free, keeping the pot small with a marginal showdown value hand.

The board is 4c 2d 9s 7s, and my hand (Qd 6d) has no made hand or meaningful draw. The pot is small (200), and Flash (BB) checked both pre-flop (implied by checking the option) and the flop, suggesting a weak range. Since I have no significant equity to improve and my bluffing range shouldn't be focused on this specific board texture, checking is the most disciplined action to keep the pot small and minimize potential losses with air.

I have bottom pair (deuces) in a limped pot. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has checked twice. While my hand has some showdown value, it is very weak. By checking behind on the turn, I control the size of the pot and move toward a cheap showdown. Betting is unlikely to get better hands to fold and might only get called by hands that beat me.

The board is 4c 2d 9s 7s 5h, which is fairly disconnected but provides several possibilities for straights (with a 3 or 8). My hand (Qd 6d) has no pair, no straight, and no flush, making it only Queen-high. Given that both my opponent and I have checked the flop and turn, it is highly likely that neither of us has a strong hand. Betting here with Queen-high would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any pair and will only get called by hands that beat me. Checking is the most disciplined and prudent action to keep the pot small and reach showdown.

I have bottom pair (deuces) in a limped pot that has been checked down to the river. The board is 4-2-9-7-5. While my hand is weak, it has significant showdown value in a pot where no one has shown any aggression. Flashlight checked on the river after checking the flop and turn. Betting is unlikely to be called by worse hands and I am unlikely to fold out better hands. Checking back for a free showdown is the most prudent play to see if my pair of deuces is good.

I am on the button with a suited connector (6d 8d), which is a hand with good playability in position. Given my stack size and position, a raise is the optimal move to try and steal the blinds or build a pot in a favorable spot. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 5h, which is a weak offsuit connector. Flashlight (BTN) has opened for 300. While the price to call is 250 into a 450 pot, playing such a weak hand out of position against two skilled opponents (Flashlight and Pro) is generally unprofitable. Furthermore, the Big Blind (Pro) is still left to act and could easily squeeze, forcing me to fold and lose the chips I've put in. I will choose the conservative and standard play here and fold.
Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, Q9o is too weak and disconnected to play profitably from out of position in the Big Blind. The best play is to simply fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Four suited, which is a strong starting hand for a three-handed game. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position with a hand that has both flush and straight potential.

3h2h is a very weak suited connector. Facing a 3x raise from the button and being out of position in the small blind, this is an easy fold. Calling here would lead to playing a very weak hand out of position post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary with this combo.

Flash has raised to 300 from the button, and the small blind has folded. With Queen-Jack offsuit in the big blind, this is a standard defensive call. I have sufficient pot odds to see a flop and continue my play against the button opener.

I have middle pair with Queen-Jack on a King-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flash), the standard play is to check to them. I have a decent hand to call a reasonable bet, but betting into the aggressor here is unnecessary and turns my hand into a bluff or commits too many chips to a marginal holding.

On the flop of 5h Kd Qd, I have an Ace-high flush draw with the Ace and Four of hearts. This board contains two broadway cards (K, Q) and two diamonds, which likely hit the Big Blind's (Flashlight) defending range. Since Flashlight checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, I have the opportunity to continue my aggression and represent a strong hand like a King or Queen while building the pot for when I potentially hit my flush. A continuation bet of roughly half the pot is standard here to apply pressure and realize my equity.

The flop of King-Queen-5 with two diamonds gives me second pair. Flash's bet of 325 into a 650 pot is a standard continuation bet. With second pair, I have sufficient equity and pot odds (25%) to call and see a turn card, keeping my range wide while not overcommitting to a marginal holding on a board where a King is likely in the opponent's range.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flash) with second pair (Queen-Jack) on a King-high board. The Turn card (8s) is relatively safe and does not significantly change the texture of the board. Since I am first to act on the turn, checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and see if my opponent intends to bet or check back to take a free river card. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or induce a raise, which I cannot comfortably call, so checking keeps the pot manageable and preserves my showdown value.

On the Turn (8s), the board remains quite heavy with Broadway cards. I still have the nut flush draw (Ace-high hearts), and my opponent has checked to me again. While a second barrel could push out weaker holdings, I have plenty of showdown value and direct equity. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity or bluff if the flush misses and the board is favorable. It also keeps the pot manageable in case Flashlight is trapping with a strong hand like a King or Queen.

I have reached the river with middle pair (Queens). The turn was checked through by both players, and my opponent's passive play suggests they likely have a marginal hand or have given up on a draw. Checking allows me to reach showdown without risking more chips, while betting here would likely only be called by hands that beat mine, such as a King, or would force them to fold hands that I currently beat. Checking is the most optimal way to realize the showdown value of my pair of Queens.

On the river (9d), the board is 5h-Kd-Qd-8s-9d. My Ace-high (Ah 4h) has very little showdown value, as almost any hand Flashlight called with on the flop (Kx, Qx, or even JT which now has a straight) beats me. The arrival of the third diamond on the river complicates things; while it's a good card to bluff on, Flashlight's check might be a trap with a flush or a marginal hand intended to induce a bluff. Given that I checked back on the turn, my hand looks capped, and a river bluff is unlikely to fold out a King or Queen. Therefore, I will check behind and hope to occasionally win against a lower Ace or a missed straight draw that didn't improve, preserving my stack as the current short stack in this three-handed game.

With King-Eight offsuit on the Button in a 3-handed game, the hand is marginal and right on the border of a steal attempt. Given our comfortable stack size and the remaining hands in the tournament, I prefer folding and waiting for a better opportunity rather than playing a slightly below-average hand out of position post-flop if called.

Pocket nines are a strong hand in a 3-handed game, especially heads-up against the Big Blind after the Button folds. Raising allows me to take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially win it pre-flop or with a continuation bet on favorable boards. A raise to 300 is a standard, aggressive sizing.

I am in the Big Blind holding K♥ 5♥. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Small Blind after the Button folded. I have position on Flashlight for the rest of the hand. Calling 200 more into a pot of 400 (getting 2:1) with a suited King is a standard defense in this spot, as the hand has decent post-flop equity and playability.

I have an overpair on a highly coordinated low board. The board texture [3s, 4d, 6d] contains straight and flush draws. I need to protect my hand by betting to make it expensive for my opponent to draw. A continuation bet of 350, which is slightly over half the pot, is a standard and effective sizing to apply pressure and define my hand strength.

I am in position against Flashlight in a blind-versus-blind battle. On a flop of 3s-4d-6d, my hand (Kh 5h) has an open-ended straight draw (any 2 or 7 completes it). Flashlight has bet 350 into a 600 pot. With 8 outs to a straight plus a potentially live overcard (King), I have sufficient equity to continue. The pot odds are roughly 2.7:1, and given the stack depths, I have good implied odds if I hit my straight on the turn or river. Calling allows me to realize my equity in position without inflating the pot too early with just a draw.

The board has become more dangerous with the King on the turn. I am out of position against the Big Blind, and my pair of nines is now an underpair to the King. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river. If my opponent checks behind, I get a free showdown; if they bet, I can make a more informed decision based on the sizing and the river card.

On the turn, I have improved to top pair (Kings) while still maintaining my open-ended straight draw (2 or 7). Flashlight's check on the King suggests weakness or a shift to a pot-control line after their flop continuation bet. With the board containing multiple draws (specifically the diamond flush draw and straight possibilities), I want to bet for value and protection. A bet of approximately half the pot is appropriate to charge drawing hands while still being small enough to get called by middle pairs or ace-high hands that may be curious.

I hold pocket nines on a board of 3s, 4d, 6d, Ks. The turn card, the King of Spades, is a very dangerous card that hits a large portion of my opponent's calling range. My opponent, Flash, decided to lead out with a bet of 700 into a pot of 1300 after I checked. While my nines are now an underpair to the King, folding here would be an overreaction. The pot odds are favorable (700 to call for a potential total pot of 2700), and my nines function as a strong bluff-catcher against semi-bluffs or lower pairs like 5-7, 7-8, or even mid-pocket pairs that might be trying to take the pot down. Calling allows me to reach the river to re-evaluate, keeping the pot size manageable while minimizing the risk of getting blown off the hand by a potential bluff.

The board has become quite coordinated with the river 7c, completing possible straight draws like 5-8 or 4-5. My pocket nines have significantly decreased in value and essentially function as a bluff-catcher. Checking is the most strategic play here; betting would likely only fold out worse hands (bluffs) that might bet if I check, and I would be called by better hands. I intend to check-call a reasonable bet or check-down if the opponent also checks.

I have top pair (Kings) with a 5 kicker on a board of 3s-4d-6d-Ks-7c. In a blind-versus-blind situation, top pair is a very strong hand. On the turn, I bet 700 after Flashlight checked, and they called. The river 7c is relatively safe, though it does complete a straight for 58 (which is unlikely but possible). Flashlight has checked to me again. While I have top pair, my kicker is extremely weak. If I bet for value, I am likely only called by better Kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) or perhaps a stubborn pocket pair like QQ or JJ. By checking back, I realize my showdown value and win against missed draws (like the diamond or spade flush draws) and middle pairs that Flashlight might have been check-calling with. Given the pot size and my weak kicker, checking is the most prudent play to secure the pot.

I am on the button with T3 suited. In a 3-handed game, the button's position is very valuable, making this a reasonable hand to play for the price of one big blind. Calling allows me to see a flop in position with the potential to outmaneuver my opponents if I hit, while keeping the pot small.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Four offsuit. Flashlight on the button has limped in, and I only need to commit 50 more chips to see the flop in a 250-chip pot. While King-Four is not a premium hand, getting 5-to-1 odds to see a flop is generally acceptable in a limped pot, especially with a King. I will call and see how the Big Blind responds.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind facing a button limp and a small blind complete. This is a premium hand that plays very well in raised pots. I want to punish the limpers, build the pot, and ideally isolate to play heads-up or win the pot pre-flop. A raise to 400 is a solid sizing to accomplish this.

While I initially limped with T3 suited, facing a 4x raise from the big blind significantly devalues this hand. T3 suited is too weak to continue against a raise, and folding allows me to preserve my stack for better spots. Being on the button is an advantage, but it does not justify calling a raise with such a marginal hand.

Pro has raised to 400 from the Big Blind after a limp and a complete. King-Four offsuit is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position against a player who has shown consistent aggression. While I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call, K4o plays poorly post-flop and is likely dominated by Pro's raising range. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Q8o. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. Raising to 300 is a standard 3x open that allows me to play the hand in position if either the small blind or big blind calls.

With Ac6c in the Small Blind facing a Button open from Flash, we are in a classic 3-bet or fold situation. A6s serves as an excellent 3-bet semi-bluff because it blocks strong Aces (like AK/AQ) and has great playability if called, thanks to its flush potential. 3-betting also forces the Big Blind out of the pot and gives us the initiative, which helps mitigate our positional disadvantage post-flop.

I hold a very weak hand (8d, 4s), which has almost no equity against the ranges of a button raise and a 3-bet from the small blind. Calling or raising here would be a significant mistake as the pot is already heavily contested by two opponents. The most disciplined and strategic play is to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I opened with a standard raise from the button with Q8o, but Pro's 3-bet from the small blind indicates significant strength. While I have position, Q8o is too weak to call a large 3-bet against a competent opponent. Continuing here would often lead to difficult spots post-flop where I am either dominated or forced to fold to further aggression. It is more prudent to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.
I am on the button with Q4o, which is a very weak, uncoordinated offsuit hand. It falls far outside any standard opening range, even 3-handed. Folding is the only sensible play here.

The hand 5d-3c is extremely weak, unsuited, and lacks significant playability, especially from the small blind position. Given the disadvantage of being out of position against the big blind for the rest of the hand, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision.

I am holding Jack-high, offsuit 8. This is a weak hand, especially in a three-handed game where position is helpful but hand strength remains paramount. With no clear equity advantage and the risk of playing a potentially bloated pot against two opponents, folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 3d. While this hand is suited and has some connectivity, it is very weak. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro) to beat. Pro has shown a willingness to fold to aggression in the past, but 53s is near the bottom of my range and playing out of position for the rest of the hand makes it difficult to realize equity. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. I want to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds.

A9 offsuit is a marginal hand to play from the Small Blind against a 3x Button open. Playing out of position with a medium kicker can lead to difficult post-flop spots, and flat-calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or overcall. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here.

6-3 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand with very little playability. Even though I am in the Big Blind and getting decent pot odds to call, playing this hand out of position against a button open is a losing proposition in the long run. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.
T6o is a very weak offsuit hand. As the first to act on the Button, this is a trivial fold to preserve our stack.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (Jack-Five offsuit). Given that the button (Pro) has folded, I could attempt a steal, but the hand is too weak to commit extra chips out of position against the Big Blind, who has been reasonably active. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent play here to avoid playing a negative expected value pot.

I am on the button with Ace-five offsuit, which is a playable hand in late position. Raising from the button allows me to potentially take down the blinds immediately or play the hand with initiative. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is a standard opening size that balances aggression with stack conservation.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Deuce offsuit. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. While King-high has some value in three-handed play, calling from the Small Blind out of position against an active button raiser with such a weak kicker is generally a losing play. Additionally, 'Pro' is still to act in the Big Blind and could easily squeeze. I will fold and wait for a better spot to preserve my stack.

With pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand in Hold'em, a 3-bet is mandatory for value and to build the pot out of position. Facing a button raise to 300, a standard 3-bet size is around 3x-4x. Raising to 1000 is consistent with my previous 3-bet sizings and puts pressure on the opponent's opening range.

I opened with Ace-Five offsuit from the button, which is standard, but facing a 3-bet (re-raise) from the big blind (Pro) is problematic. A5o performs poorly against a tight 3-betting range, and I am not deep enough to justify a loose call in hopes of hitting a miracle. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While it is a marginal hand that could sometimes be used for a steal, the current table dynamics show that both Pro and Flashlight are willing to defend their blinds and play aggressively. With a stack of 80 big blinds, there is no need to play such a weak hand out of position post-flop or risk a three-bet from the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 3d, which is a very weak offsuit connector. Even in a blind-versus-blind situation, playing this hand out of position is unprofitable. Folding is the best decision to conserve chips.

I have Pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. As the first to act on the Button, I will make a standard opening raise to build the pot while disguising my exact hand strength. A 2.5x raise (250) is a standard opening size that I've used previously with a wide range of hands, encouraging calls from weaker holdings.

I am holding 4-3 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. Being in the small blind against a button open makes this an easy fold. There is no strategic value in defending this hand out of position against a range that is likely stronger than mine.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven suited. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard raise to 250. With the Small Blind folding, I only need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me excellent pot odds (nearly 3:1) and Ten-Seven suited is a hand that retains equity well and plays effectively post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the opponent's range, calling to defend my blind is the most profitable long-term play.

I have flopped a backdoor flush draw and one overcard (the Ten) to the bottom card of the board. The board texture (Q-J-4) significantly favors the Button's pre-flop raising range, which likely includes many Queens, Jacks, and high-card combinations. As I am out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor and see their continuation bet sizing before deciding whether to continue with my backdoor equity.

I have an overpair (Pocket Aces) on a wet board featuring straight and flush draws (Qc Jd 4c). Betting is essential to extract value from worse made hands like top pair (Qx) or middle pair (Jx), and to charge draws to see the turn. A bet of around half the pot accomplishes this.

On a board of Queen-Jack-Four, the texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes many broadway cards and high pairs. Holding Ten-Seven of diamonds, I have only ten-high and a backdoor diamond flush draw. I am out of position against the Button (Pro), who has followed up their pre-flop raise with a 300-chip continuation bet into a 550-chip pot. Given my lack of direct equity (no pair, no straight draw) and the likelihood that my opponent has connected with this board or has overcards with significantly more equity, calling here would be a mistake. The pot odds are roughly 2.8:1, but my hand is too weak to realize equity out of position on such a coordinated board. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the button position holding 3s 9h, which is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low equity. Investing 100 chips to play this hand is mathematically unprofitable, so folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of Threes (3c, 3d). The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). Pocket Threes are a solid starting hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Given that it's only 50 chips more to call, and raising is the standard play here to seize the initiative and potentially end the hand immediately, I will raise to 300. This is a standard 3x open from the SB to put pressure on the Big Blind.

AQo is a premium hand in a blind versus blind situation. Flash opened to 3x from the Small Blind, and 3-betting here is the best way to extract value, deny equity, and take the initiative in the hand. I will use a 3-bet size of 1000, which has been my standard 3-bet sizing against a 300 open.

I am holding pocket Threes in the Small Blind. After my initial raise to 300, the Big Blind (Pro) has 3-bet to 1000. This is the third time Pro has 3-bet me in this specific spot (previously Hand #18 and Hand #87), suggesting a high level of aggression or a polarized range. Calling 700 more into a pot of 1300 gives me decent pot odds, and with effective stacks at approximately 7500, the implied odds for set-mining are just above 10:1. While playing a small pair out of position is challenging, calling here prevents Pro from automatically winning every time they 3-bet my opens. I intend to fold on the flop if I do not improve to a set or if the board texture is extremely unfavorable.

On a dry flop of 7h-4s-9d, my pocket Threes are an underpair to the board. Having called a 3-bet out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Pro's range for 3-betting often includes high overcards (like AK, AQ) and overpairs (88+). While this board doesn't hit high cards well, Pro is likely to continuation bet. By checking, I can gauge the strength of Pro's hand based on their betting size and potentially see a turn card cheaply if they decide to check back. I do not have enough strength to lead out into the aggressor here.

On a dry, disconnected rainbow board (7h 4s 9d), I have a strong range advantage as the pre-flop 3-bettor. My Ace-Queen high has decent equity, but a continuation bet will fold out the opponent's worse overcards, deny equity to various floats, and potentially fold out small pocket pairs. A small c-bet of around 1/3 of the pot is standard here to maintain aggression and represent an overpair.

On this dry flop of 7h-4s-9d, I am holding an underpair (pocket Threes). Pre-flop, Pro 3-bet me to 1000, which I called out of position. Pro has now followed up with a continuation bet of 700 into a 2000 chip pot (35% pot). While the price to call is relatively attractive (roughly 3.85:1), my hand is extremely vulnerable. Pro's 3-betting range likely consists of high overpairs (TT+), which have me crushed, or high overcards (AK, AQ, AJ) that still have significant equity against me and will likely barrel on the turn. Without a 3 on the turn, I will be forced to guess on almost every card. Given that I am out of position and have a very weak holding relative to a 3-bet pot range, I will fold now to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot to challenge Pro's aggression.

I am on the button with 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With two active players in the blinds and no reason to attempt a high-risk steal with such poor equity, folding is the standard and most prudent play.

I am in the Small Blind with Q5o, a weak offsuit hand. The Button has folded, so it's just me and the Big Blind. Q5o plays poorly post-flop out of position, and there is no compelling reason to steal or defend with this hand here. Folding is the most standard and profitable long-term play.

I am dealt Six-Deuce suited on the Button. This is an extremely weak hand with very low equity and poor playability post-flop. Even though I have position, opening with such a weak hand is likely a losing play in the long run. Folding is the best decision.

I am in the small blind with a speculative suited hand (Jh5h). Completing the small blind to see a flop is a standard play given the pot odds, allowing me to potentially hit the board cheaply against the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind has just completed the blind, and the Button folded. While King-Nine is a relatively strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, checking allows me to see a free flop and utilize my positional advantage post-flop. This keeps the pot small and minimizes risk with a marginal hand.

I have flopped top pair (Jacks) on a relatively dry board. With only one opponent left (Flash), betting half the pot (100) is a solid standard play to test the strength of my opponent's hand and protect my pair from potential overcards.

In this blind-versus-blind situation, I checked pre-flop with King-Nine offsuit. The flop of Jack-Five-Four does not connect with my hand in any significant way, leaving me with only King-high. Flashlight has led out with a half-pot bet. While the pot odds of 4:1 are somewhat tempting for a call to see if I can improve on the turn, I have no direct draws (only two overcards) and no backdoor potential other than the King of spades for a runner-runner flush. Given the board texture and the fact that I am completely missing, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack rather than floating with marginal equity.

The hand 4-7 offsuit is among the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Regardless of my position as the button, there is no strategic advantage to playing such a hand. It lacks connectivity, high card strength, and suitedness, making it highly unlikely to improve to a winning hand. Folding is the standard, disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 6s. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). I need to call 50 more to see a flop. While 46 offsuit is a weak hand, getting 3-to-1 odds to call in a blind-vs-blind situation is occasionally acceptable if I intend to play post-flop. However, 'Pro' has been fairly aggressive and my hand is quite poor. In a three-handed high-roller tournament, I should prioritize maintaining my stack and avoiding marginal spots out of position. I will fold this hand to wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 7h 3h. This is a very weak hand. While I could potentially steal the blinds with a raise, my opponents (Pro and Flashlight) have been playing relatively active and have large stacks. Folding here is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my chips for better opportunities.
Pocket Jacks is a premium hand. With the button folding, I am first to act against the Big Blind. I want to build the pot and not give a free flop, so raising to 3 big blinds is standard.

Holding 9-3 offsuit, I have a very weak hand with extremely low playability. Even though the pot odds for calling the 200 chip raise are decent, defending the big blind with such a marginal holding against an aggressive opponent like Pro is negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined and correct strategic decision here.

JTs is a very strong speculative hand and an excellent candidate to open from the Button. Raising to 2.5x the big blind (250) is a standard open size that puts pressure on the small and big blinds to fold, while still allowing us to play a reasonable pot in position if we get called.

Pro is an aggressive opener on the button. Holding 6s5s, a suited connector, I have decent playability and implied odds. With the pot already at 400 and a requirement of 200 to call, the pot odds are sufficient to warrant a call to see a flop, despite being out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s7s, a decent suited gapper. The Button (Pro) raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. The pot is currently 600, and I only need to call 150 more to see a flop. This gives me excellent pot odds of 4:1 to close the action. While I will be out of position post-flop, the hand has good multi-way potential and the price is too attractive to fold. Raising is an option but calling preserves my stack and allows me to see if I connect with the board before committing more chips against the two larger stacks.

I have bottom pair on a board with two high overcards and potential straight and flush draws. With three players in the pot, my hand has very limited showdown value and is vulnerable to many turn cards. Checking is the most prudent action to see how the other players react without overcommitting to a weak pair.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s7s. The flop is 6c Jd Qd. This board is quite coordinated and hits the pre-flop raiser's range (Pro) and the caller's range (Flashlight) much harder than mine. I have missed completely, having only back-door straight possibilities and no draws. Flashlight has already checked. Since I am out of position against both players and have no current value or significant equity, I will check to see what the original raiser (Pro) does. There is no need to bloat the pot with air in a three-way situation.

With middle pair and a marginal kicker on a draw-heavy board, checking behind for pot control is the standard play in a multi-way pot. A bet is unlikely to get value from worse hands and would mainly isolate us against stronger hands like top pair, better jacks, or strong combo draws.

The board is now extremely coordinated following the turn King of Diamonds, creating a high-card heavy texture that likely hits the ranges of my opponents. My bottom pair of sixes has almost zero showdown value against two opponents in a multi-way pot. Betting would serve no purpose as I will only be called by hands that are already crushing me. Checking is the only prudent action to minimize potential losses and keep the hand manageable.

The turn brings the King of Diamonds, making the board Queen-Jack-King-6. This board is extremely well-connected and likely hits the range of either the pre-flop raiser (Pro) or the caller (Flashlight). I have no pair and no draw. Since Flashlight has checked to me, checking is the most prudent action. I have zero equity in this pot and no reason to attempt a bluff into two opponents on such a coordinated board.

With third pair and an open-ended straight draw on a very wet board (three diamonds, four to a straight), checking for pot control and taking a free card is the best option. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands or draws with more equity, and we don't want to build a big pot or face a check-raise when we have a marginal made hand but decent drawing equity.

The board has become extremely coordinated, featuring Kings, Queens, and Jacks. My pair of sixes is essentially a bluff catcher at best, and has almost zero showdown value in a three-way pot. Given that the board has not improved my hand and there are multiple overcards, betting would likely only be called by hands that have me beat. Checking is the most prudent action to minimize potential losses and see if the hand makes it to showdown without any further investment.

The board is 6c Jd Qd Kd Kh, and I hold 7s 9s, which is 9-high. The action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, and now Flashlight has checked the river. I have no showdown value and no piece of this board. While there's a small chance my 9-high could win against a totally missed hand, betting as a bluff into two opponents on a board with three broadway cards and a paired King is unlikely to succeed. I will check and hope the Button (Pro) also checks, giving me a free showdown, though I expect to lose.
