On this highly coordinated board with three diamonds and a four-to-a-straight texture (J-Q-K-K), our pair of Jacks gives us two pair (Kings and Jacks with a Queen kicker). Any bet here would almost certainly only be called or raised by better hands, such as a Queen, a King, a flush, or a straight. Since both opponents checked, we likely have enough showdown value to beat missed straight or flush draws, as well as smaller pairs. Checking behind is the safest and most optimal play to realize our showdown equity.

I am on the button with suited connector Jd7d. This is a playable hand in position. The blinds are 50/100. Raising to 300 is a standard open that allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or define the range of the blinds, leveraging my position for the post-flop play.

I am in the Small Blind with Th9h, which is a strong suited connector. Flashlight (Button) raised to 300. This is a standard button open. With a stack of 6325, I have plenty of room to play post-flop. Calling here is standard to realize the equity of a hand that flops well, though I must be careful being out of position. Raising is also an option, but given the stack sizes and the strength of Th9h, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 65o, a weak offsuit connector. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, playing this hand out of position multi-way is unprofitable. Despite the decent pot odds, the hand has poor equity realization and high reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and most optimal play here.

On this flop of Kh-Ts-Qd, I have middle pair (tens) and an open-ended straight draw (any Jack gives me a straight). This is a very strong drawing hand, but the board is extremely coordinated and hits the pre-flop raiser's range very well. Being out of position against Flashlight, I should start with a check to see how they react to this texture. If they bet, I have enough equity with the pair plus open-ended straight draw to continue.

The flop is Kh-Ts-Qd, which is quite coordinated and likely connects with the opponent's range. My Jack gives me a gutshot straight draw, needing either an Ace or a Nine. A continuation bet of 350, which is half the pot, is a standard strategic move here to maintain the initiative I gained pre-flop, apply pressure, and potentially take down the pot immediately or build a larger pot if I hit my straight.

On a flop of Kh-Ts-Qd, I have middle pair (tens) and an open-ended straight draw (any Jack provides a straight). Flashlight has bet 350 into a 700-chip pot (1/2 pot). While this board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (containing many Kx, Qx, and high pairs), the strength of my draw combined with my current pair makes this an easy call. I am receiving 3:1 pot odds (350 to win 1050) and have roughly 8 clean outs for the nuts plus additional outs to improve my pair. Raising is too risky given how well this board hits Flashlight's range, so I will call to realize my equity and evaluate on the turn.

On this turn, the Ace of Diamonds is a very significant card. It completes a broadway straight for any player holding a Jack (J), and it also improves any Ace-x hands in Flashlight's button-opening range to top pair. My hand, a pair of Tens, has now significantly dropped in relative strength. I do not have a Jack in my hand, so I am not holding the straight. I am currently out of position and the board is extremely coordinated. The best play here is to check and see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts to the Ace. If they bet, I will likely have to fold given the number of hands in their range that now beat me (any J, any A, any K, any Q).

I have hit the Broadway straight on the turn (A-K-Q-J-T). The board is Kh-Ts-Qd-Ad. I have the absolute nuts. The pot is 1400. I need to bet for value to get paid by any potential sets, two pairs, or weaker hands. Betting 700 (half-pot) is a solid, standard value bet size in this scenario to extract chips while keeping the pot manageable for the river.

The board texture (Kh Ts Qd Ad) is extremely dangerous for my hand. Any player holding a Jack now has the broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T). Even if Flashlight does not have a Jack, my pair of Tens is currently losing to any Ace, King, or Queen, all of which are highly likely to be in the Button's range. Flashlight has bet 700 into a 1400 pot on the turn, showing continued aggression on a card (the Ace) that likely improved their hand or completed a draw. I have no significant outs to improve to a winning hand that doesn't also improve my opponent's range. Continuing with the fourth-best pair on this board is not profitable. I will fold.

3h 2d is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I am on the button and have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand, this hand has virtually no post-flop playability and very little equity against any range the blinds will defend with. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities in the remaining 48 hands.

With Q5s in the Small Blind after the Button folds, I have a marginal but playable suited hand. Limping (completing the blind) allows me to see a flop cheaply, keeping the pot small while retaining equity, and avoids committing too many chips against a potential raise from the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The button folded, and the small blind completed. I have a decent hand, but out of position, I prefer to check and see a flop for free to keep the pot controlled rather than bloating it with a marginal holding.
I completely missed the flop and have no pair or viable draws on this Ace-high board. Checking is the standard play here to evaluate the Big Blind's action; I intend to fold to any bet.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Ten offsuit on an Ace-high board. I do not have a pair or a strong draw, only a backdoor straight draw. Since the small blind (Pro) checked the flop, I will check behind to keep the pot small and see a free turn card. There is no reason to bet as a bluff here, as I have sufficient showdown value to potentially check down if it remains unaggressive, and betting would likely only get called by better hands.
With no pair and no draw on this board, I still have nothing but Queen high. Checking is the only sensible option, and I will fold if my opponent decides to bet.

The board shows 6h-7c-As-9h. I have Qh-Tc, which is currently just Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). Pro has checked both the flop and the turn, signaling a likely weak hand or trap. With no pair and a tiny pot, there is no value in betting as a bluff, as it won't fold out better hands. Checking behind is the most disciplined play to control the pot and see the river for free.

I missed the board entirely and have Queen high. Since my opponent checked down the whole way, they likely have a weak pair or a high card. Bluffing here is unnecessary in such a small limped pot. Checking gives me a chance to see a free showdown where Queen high might occasionally be good against missed draws, and I can safely fold if they happen to bet.

I have Queen-high on a board of Ace-Ace-Nine-Seven-Six. Pro checked the river, continuing the pattern of checking through every street. My hand has no value as a bet because any hand that Pro would call with would beat my Queen-high, and I do not need to bluff against a player who has shown no interest in betting. Checking behind is the optimal play to reach showdown with the hope that Queen-high is sufficient or simply to conclude the hand without risking more chips.
I am on the button with 7-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Without any premium value or playability post-flop, folding is the only profitable long-term play here.
With a very weak hand of 2d 9c, there is no strategic value in completing the small blind and playing out of position. The hand has no equity, so folding is the standard and most profitable decision.

I am holding 3d 5s, which is an extremely weak starting hand with very little potential for improvement or value. Playing this hand would be a negative expected value decision. As the button, I have the option to fold, call, or raise, but given the poor quality of my hole cards and the lack of any significant initiative, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am in the Small Blind with Js 7s. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro). Js 7s is a strong enough hand to play aggressively in a 3-handed game, especially from the Small Blind. I will raise to 300 to either take the blinds immediately or play the hand with the initiative. Pro has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop three-bets and raises in previous hands, so a standard raise here is a profitable move.

Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, Q4 suited is a weak hand with limited playability post-flop despite having position. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 7-5 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. Although it is a 3-handed game where ranges are wider, both of my opponents have significantly larger stacks and have shown a willingness to play back at my raises in previous hands (e.g., Hand #87 and Hand #95). Folding is the safest and most standard play here to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with 84 offsuit, which is a very weak and disconnected hand. Flash folded on the button. There is no reason to limp or raise with this hand, so folding is the optimal and standard play.

With pocket deuces on the button, I have a clear opportunity to open-raise. A standard raise to 2.5x (250) applies pressure on the blinds, allowing me to potentially win the pot uncontested. If called, I can play for set value post-flop.

I am in the small blind with Jack-Eight offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against a raise from the button. Pro has been an active player, but there is no need to defend this marginal hand out of position when I can wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Q9o. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of roughly 2.6 to 1 (requiring about 27% equity). Q9o is a standard hand to defend in the Big Blind against a wide Button opening range, as it retains decent equity and playability post-flop. I will call to see the flop.

I have flopped trip Queens with a 9 kicker on a Qd-Js-Qs board. This is an exceptionally strong hand. In this heads-up situation against the pre-flop raiser (Pro), the standard play is to check to the aggressor. By checking, I allow Pro to follow through with a continuation bet using his entire range, including bluffs, pocket pairs, or a Jack. Leading out ('donk betting') might fold out his weaker hands immediately. I will check and plan to either call or raise depending on his bet sizing and player tendencies.

The Qd Js Qs board connects heavily with the Big Blind's calling range, which likely includes Qx, Jx, broadway straight draws, and flush draws. My pocket deuces are currently very weak and have almost no equity against a calling range. Betting here as a bluff or for protection is risky as I will often get called or check-raised. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, avoid unnecessary risk, and see a free turn card, potentially hitting a 2-outer.

I have trip Queens with a 9 kicker. On the Qd-Js-Qs flop, both I and the pre-flop raiser (Pro) checked. The turn is the Ace of Hearts, which is a card that significantly interacts with a Button opening range (containing many Ax and KT hands). By checking a second time, I am continuing to play my monster hand deceptively. If Pro has picked up a pair of Aces or has a hand like Ace-Jack, he is very likely to bet for value now. If he has nothing, checking gives him the opportunity to bluff at a pot that looks like nobody wants it. I will check and plan to call or raise depending on Pro's action.

The board is incredibly hostile for pocket deuces (Q-J-Q-A). Our hand has virtually zero equity against any part of Flash's calling range, and even his missed draws or floats likely have high-card value that beats our pair. While Flash has checked twice, firing a delayed c-bet bluff on this turn is very risky given how heavily this board texture favors a Big Blind flatting range. With no blockers and no natural equity to fall back on, checking again is the most prudent play. It keeps the pot small and allows us to realize what little equity we have, accepting that we are basically giving up on the hand unless we spike a miracle two on the river.

I have trip Queens with a 9 kicker on a board of Qd-Js-Qs-Ah-3d. Both the flop and turn were checked through, which suggests that Pro likely does not have a monster hand like JJ, AA, or AQ, as he probably would have continuation bet the flop or bet the turn when the Ace landed. However, Pro could easily have a Jack, a pocket pair, or a weak Ace that he is looking to take to showdown cheaply. By leading out on the river for 300 (slightly more than half the pot), I am attempting to extract value from those marginal hands. A river lead after checking twice often looks like a stab at a 'dead' pot, which may induce a call from a player of Pro's caliber holding any piece of the board.

The board is Qd Js Qs Ah 3d. I hold pocket 2s. The opponent (Flash) bet 300 into 550 on the river after it checked down. My pair of deuces is hopelessly crushed by almost any holding the opponent could have, even missed draws, as they likely have high cards (e.g., K-high or 10-high) or paired the board. I have no showdown value and attempting a bluff raise is extremely risky and unnecessary. The only logical play is to fold.

I am on the button with K4o. The pot is currently 150. Flash posted the Small Blind and Pro posted the Big Blind. I have to call 100 to stay in the hand. K4o is a very weak hand, generally not worth playing, but since I am on the button and the cost to call is only 100, checking or folding are the usual options. However, I have already folded many weak hands, and the stack size is healthy. Given the position, I'll fold as this is a marginal hand that will rarely improve significantly enough to win a pot without a strong read, and I don't want to get involved in a bloated pot with a dominated King.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Three offsuit (Q3o), which is a very weak hand. Flashlight (the Button) has folded. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, I would be playing out of position against a competent opponent (Pro) in the Big Blind. Q3o does not play well post-flop and rarely wins a significant pot. In a three-handed game, I prefer to fold my weakest hands from the Small Blind to avoid being exploited by a strong Big Blind.

I am on the button with 6c 3c. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button typically opens a wide range, 63s is at the very bottom and doesn't play well against the aggressive styles of Pro and Flashlight, who have both shown willingness to defend their blinds and 3-bet. Given that I am currently the shortest stack at the table, I will preserve my chips and wait for a better spot.

QJo is a strong starting hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. After the Button folds, raising from the Small Blind puts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to take down the pot pre-flop or build it with a solid holding.

K5o is a very weak hand to defend the big blind with, especially out of position against a raise from the small blind. While the pot odds are decent, playing a weak hand like this out of position is generally negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve the stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 6d 7c, an unsuited and weak connector. This hand has very poor playability and equity, making it a clear fold pre-flop. There's no need to attempt a steal with such weak holdings.

With J-2 offsuit, this is a weak hand even in the small blind position. Given the stack depths and the poor quality of the cards, I prefer to fold and preserve my chips rather than playing a marginal hand out of position.

I am holding a very weak hand, 9-2 offsuit, on the button. There is no strategic value in playing this hand, and since I am not obligated to put more chips in the pot, folding is the correct and disciplined decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 8d after the Button has folded. In a blind-versus-blind situation, 98 offsuit is a strong enough hand to either complete or raise. Given that Pro (the Big Blind) has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop aggression in several recent hands, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to attempt to win the blinds immediately. If called, the hand has decent connectivity for post-flop play.

With King-Deuce offsuit, my hand is too weak to defend against a 3x raise from the Small Blind. Even though I would have position post-flop, K2o plays very poorly and is heavily dominated by Flash's raising range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Qs 2s. In a three-handed game, the button should open a wide range of hands to put pressure on the blinds. Although Q2s is relatively weak, the power of position and the potential for a flush or top-pair high-kicker scenario make it a standard opening hand. I have noticed both opponents are willing to fold to pre-flop aggression in recent hands. I will raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds or play the pot with positional advantage.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, QJo is a marginal offsuit broadway. Given the positional disadvantage and the fact that we are deep-stacked and currently leading, there is no need to defend or 3-bet with a hand that can easily be dominated by the Button's continuing range. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit. K-To is a standard hand to defend against a button raise, offering good playability post-flop. With the pot at 450 and only 200 more needed to call, calling is the most profitable and balanced approach.
I have middle pair on an Ace-high, somewhat coordinated board. As the Big Blind acting first post-flop, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and evaluate the aggressor's intent.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I am following up with a continuation bet on the flop. While the board is coordinated and likely connects with the Big Blind's range, Flashlight's check allows me to maintain initiative and represent a strong hand like an Ace. I will bet 300 to attempt to win the pot immediately, though I will likely check down or fold to resistance on future streets if I do not improve.

The opponent has made a continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 950. Although the board is quite coordinated with an Ace present, which puts my pair of Tens at risk against many better hands, the pot odds offered are approximately 3:1. Folding here would be overly tight given the small sizing of the bet. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate my position, as I have sufficient equity to continue against the opponent's wider continuation betting range.

I hold middle pair (Tens) on an Ace-high, somewhat coordinated board. After calling the flop bet and checking the turn, checking the river is the most prudent action. My hand has showdown value but is not strong enough to value bet, as it would likely only be called by better hands. By checking, I induce potential bluffs from missed draws and reach showdown efficiently without inflating a pot I cannot comfortably win if I am behind.

I have picked up bottom pair on the turn, which gives me some marginal showdown value. However, the board is quite coordinated with several straight and flush draws present. Flashlight's check-call on the flop indicates they likely have a piece of the board (such as a Ten or Nine) or a strong draw. Betting now is unlikely to force a fold from better hands and risks a check-raise. I will check behind to keep the pot small and see the river for free, hoping to either improve further or win at showdown against missed draws.

The board has become quite connected with the Queen on the river, making it dangerous for my pair of Tens. My opponent showed aggression on the flop but checked the turn, which could indicate a weak hand, a missed draw, or a trap. With my pair of Tens, I have some showdown value but cannot value bet as I would only be called by better hands. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown and control the pot size.

On the river, the board has completed with a Queen of Diamonds, giving me second pair (Queens) with a Deuce kicker. Flashlight, the Big Blind, has checked for the third time this hand. My check on the turn likely signaled some weakness or a marginal hand, which might induce them to check down or call with a wider range of hands they think are beat. While I have improved to a pair of Queens, the board is very connected (KJ or KJ-style straights completed). Betting here is risky because better hands like any King or any Ace (which they could have check-called with on the flop) won't fold, and it's hard to get called by worse hands given the board texture. Checking behind ensures I get to showdown with my marginal pair and avoids getting check-raised off the best hand if Flashlight is trapping or missed a draw.
J2s is a very weak hand, even from the Button. Folding is the best choice here.

Pro has folded on the button, leaving a heads-up situation in the blinds. I am in the small blind with King-Five suited. This is a playable hand in a heads-up scenario, and calling the extra 50 to see a flop is a reasonable, low-risk strategy to potentially hit a strong pair or a flush draw.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Seven of Diamonds. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has completed the blind, and I have the option to check or raise. 10-7 suited is a decent hand in a limped pot, but there is no strong reason to bloat the pot here against a stack that is larger than mine. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and realize my equity while keeping the pot small.

I have flopped trip Kings with a 5 kicker on a dry board of 3c, Kd, Kc. This is a very strong hand. Since my opponent checked the flop, I should start betting for value to build the pot. A bet of 150 into the 200 pot should get value from weaker kings, pairs, or speculative hands, while remaining a standard sizing for this situation.

On a board of 3c Kd Kc, I have Ten-high with no draws. Flashlight, who limped from the Small Blind, has led out for 150 into a 200 pot. This bet suggests they either have a piece of the board (like a King or a 3) or are attempting to take the pot on a dry texture. With no equity and very few ways to improve my hand on the turn, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Holding 2-4 suited on the button is a very weak hand. Even though I am in position, the strength of the cards is insufficient to warrant a call of 100 chips against two opponents. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand given the current pot odds and my stack size.

I am in the Small Blind with K6s, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. With Flashlight folded, it is down to me and Pro in the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and represent a strong hand, which is standard for this position and holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five offsuit (T5o), which is a very weak hand. Flash, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300. There is no strategic reason to defend such a weak holding, even in a blind versus blind scenario, especially when I have a healthy stack of 12,350 and can wait for much better opportunities to commit chips.

I am on the button with Queen-Nine suited, which is a very strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Standard strategy dictates an opening raise from this position to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I have been consistently raising to 300 in previous hands with similar holdings, so I will maintain that sizing here.

34 suited is too weak to call a 3bb raise from the small blind. Being out of position against the button and with the big blind still to act, folding is the standard and mathematically sound decision.

4-9 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very little potential. Even with the discounted price to call, the hand performs poorly against an opening range. Folding is the prudent play to preserve the stack for higher equity situations.
T7o is a very weak offsuit hand. As the first to act pre-flop on the Button in a 3-handed game, the standard and most profitable decision is to fold and preserve my stack for better starting hands.

I hold pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With the button folding, I am now heads-up against the big blind. To maximize value and take control of the pot, I will raise to 300, which is the standard opening size based on my previous play.

In Hand #115, I am in the Big Blind with Ad-5h. The Button (Pro) has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. This is a common steal attempt from the small blind. Ad-5h is well above the average hand range for this situation. Calling the 200 is definitely profitable given my stack size (6975) and the pot odds. While I could potentially 3-bet to try and take the pot down immediately, Flashlight has shown aggression and sometimes calls 3-bets (as seen in Hand #37). With an Ace-high hand that has some wheel potential, calling and seeing a flop is a solid play that allows me to evaluate the strength of my hand against his wide SB range without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

I have pocket Aces on a dry, rainbow board (2h-4c-Th). This is an excellent texture for my hand as it is very unlikely my opponent connected strongly with this flop. I need to begin building the pot to maximize value against potential tens, smaller pocket pairs, or drawing hands. A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a standard continuation bet that should keep my opponent engaged without folding out their weaker range too quickly.

In Hand #115, I am in the Big Blind with Ad-5h. The flop is 2h-4c-Th. Flashlight, the Small Blind, has led out with a bet of 300 into a 600 pot. I currently have Ace-high, which could still be the best hand given Flashlight's wide opening range from the Small Blind. Additionally, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Trey for a wheel) and two overcards to the 2 and 4. With pot odds of 3:1, calling is a standard play to see if the turn improves my hand or if Flashlight slows down, allowing me to potentially take the pot later.

The board is 2h-4c-Th-6d. I hold pocket Aces (AhAc), which remains a very strong hand. The turn 6d is a safe card that does not significantly change the texture of the board. I have shown strength by betting the flop, and I will continue to bet for value to extract chips from T-x hands or medium pairs that may have called on the flop. A 600-chip bet (half the pot) is appropriate to keep building the pot while maintaining pressure.

On the turn 6d, the board is 2h-4c-Th-6d. I hold Ad-5h, giving me Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3). Flashlight has led out for 600 into an 1800 pot, offering me 4:1 pot odds. Given that I am in position and Flashlight's sizing is relatively small (1/3 pot), calling is justified. I have roughly 10 potential outs (4 threes, 3 aces, and 3 fives) that could give me the best hand, and even if I don't improve, being in position allows me to potentially take the pot on the river if Flashlight checks or to see a cheap showdown with Ace-high.

The board has developed into 2h-4c-Th-6d-3d, which completes a possible straight for any 5. While pocket Aces is a strong hand, the board texture is now dangerous, and my opponent has called bets on both the flop and turn, indicating they likely have at least a pair or a straight draw. Checking behind is the most prudent decision to reach showdown; betting here would only likely be called by hands that have me beat (such as a 5 for a straight, or possibly sets or two-pairs), while checking allows me to potentially win against any bluffs or weaker pairs my opponent might be holding.

On the river, the 3d completes my gutshot straight (A-2-3-4-5). Flashlight, who led pre-flop, on the flop, and on the turn, has now checked. This check indicates either a slowing down with a medium-strength hand (like a Ten or a pocket pair) or a complete air ball. Since I have a very strong hand (the straight), I must bet for value. A bet of 1200 (half the pot) is sized to look like a potential bluff while still being a price that one-pair hands or even a stubborn Ace-high might consider calling, maximizing my return on this hand.

The opponent has bet 1200 into a pot of 2400 on the river. My pocket Aces are a strong bluff-catcher. The board (2h-4c-Th-6d-3d) does present a straight possibility (any 5 makes the straight), but it is a relatively specific range. Given that I have shown strength throughout the hand and the pot odds (calling 1200 to win 3600) are favorable, folding is likely too weak, and the opponent may be betting with a pair of Tens or a missed draw, or even trying to take the pot with a small bet expecting me to fold due to the straight board. I will make the call.

I am in the button position with Jack-Nine offsuit. This is a weak starting hand with low potential to hit strong pairs or connectors. Given the stage of the tournament (Hand 116/150) and the marginal strength of these hole cards, folding is the most disciplined and strategically sound decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h3c, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has already folded, and I am only faced with a 50-chip call to see a flop. However, my opponent in the Big Blind is 'Pro', who has been playing a relatively solid game and currently holds the chip lead. 93 offsuit is nearly bottom-tier in terms of playability. While the price to call is small, folding here is the standard disciplined play to avoid being out of position with a marginal hand against a strong opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I am on the button with Jc7c, a decent suited semi-connector. In a three-handed game, this hand is strong enough to play, especially since the stack sizes are relatively deep (around 80-120 big blinds). Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or play in position post-flop if called. I'll make a standard opening raise to 3x the big blind.

Facing a button raise with an offsuit King and a weak kicker (K7o) from the small blind. Playing this hand out of position against the pre-flop aggressor is unprofitable, as it's easily dominated and difficult to realize equity with. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong holding in a heads-up pot against the button. Flash has been aggressive, and I should take control of the hand by 3-betting to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop or define his range. A raise to 900 is an appropriate sizing to apply pressure.

I opened to 300 with Jc7c from the button, and Flashlight responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the big blind. While J7s has some playability and I would have position for the rest of the hand, it is generally too weak to continue against a 3-bet. Flashlight has shown a capacity for aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #13, Hand #61), and calling here would likely lead to a difficult post-flop situation in a bloated pot. To preserve my stack and wait for a higher-equity spot, I will fold.

With King-Deuce offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, the hand is simply too weak to open. It lacks post-flop playability and the kicker is extremely vulnerable if I pair my King. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the small blind with 9c 4c. This is a very weak hand in a heads-up situation against an aggressive opponent. The pot is small, and there is no reason to invest further chips into such a marginal holding. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7c5h, which is a weak hand. I have already posted a 50 chip blind and the action is currently on me as the button. With 75 remaining to call, the pot odds are okay, but this hand has very poor playability. Given my stack size and the strength of the hand, I should fold to conserve chips and wait for a better opportunity, especially since I am out of position post-flop if I call.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 3d. Flashlight has folded. The action is on me. Pro is in the Big Blind and has a larger stack than me. While 63s is a suited connector, it is very weak. I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, but playing out of position against a strong opponent with such a weak hand is generally not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9-deuce suited. While suited, this is one of the weakest hands in the range. Given the stack sizes and the tendency of both opponents to defend or 3-bet, opening with such a weak hand is likely to lead to a loss of chips. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 3h, which is a very weak hand. Even though it is folded to me, playing 83s out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. I will fold and preserve my chip lead.

Jack-Deuce offsuit is a premium candidate for the muck. No reason to play this junk, especially considering I'm the chip leader and have no need to force action with weak cards. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up scenario as the small blind, 9-7 offsuit is a marginal hand, but given the small cost to complete the blind (50 chips to potentially see a flop in a pot of 150), it is reasonable to call and see a flop.

I have been dealt pocket sixes in the big blind. After the button folds and the small blind only completes the bet, I have a strong hand for three-handed play. I will raise to 400 to take control of the pot and define the strength of my hand, rather than checking and allowing the small blind to see a flop for free with potentially overcards.

With 9-7 offsuit, I have a weak marginal hand out of position against a raise from the Big Blind. Calling an additional 350 chips into this pot is negative expected value, so the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 7c 3d, which is an extremely weak hand with very little potential to improve or win a showdown. Defending my position on the button with such a poor starting hand is not statistically sound. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger holding.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Flashlight folded, so I am heads-up against Pro who is in the Big Blind. A-5o is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind and win the pot immediately or maintain the initiative. Pro has a larger stack, but I have a high enough stack to comfortably raise. I'll make it 3x the big blind (300 total).

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand (7d 4c). Flash raised from the Small Blind. There is no strategic reason to defend the blind or play a pot with such poor cards. Folding is the obvious and optimal choice.

I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. With a stack of over 90 big blinds and 27 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

With 97s in the small blind and the button folded, this is a great hand to open with a raise. It's a suited connector/one-gapper that plays well post-flop, and a standard 3x raise to 300 applies pressure on the big blind to potentially win the pot right now.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind, I need to call 200 to see the flop. Given the pot odds and my holding an Ace, defending is a standard play. Raising A-2 offsuit out of position against a potentially strong range is risky, so I will call and evaluate the flop.

On a board of 4h Kh 6s, my 7h 9h gives me a strong flush draw and a gutshot straight draw. As the pre-flop raiser, leading out with a continuation bet applies pressure to fold out better high-card hands or weak pairs, while also building the pot in case my draw hits. A half-pot sizing is standard and accomplishes both goals effectively.

I have the nut flush draw on a rainbow board with two hearts. The pot is 600 and the bet is 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. With the Ace of hearts, I have strong equity to improve to the best hand and implied odds to win a large pot if I hit my flush. Calling is the standard, high-percentage play to realize my equity and keep the opponent's range wide, as raising might force folds from weaker hands I dominate or bluff catchers I want to keep in.

The Ace on the turn is an excellent card to double-barrel, as it heavily favors my perceived pre-flop raising range. By continuing to bet, I put maximum pressure on the big blind to fold out marginal hands like a weak King or middle pair. Furthermore, I still retain a strong heart flush draw, giving me significant equity to improve to the winning hand on the river if called. A half-pot bet sizes perfectly to balance risk and fold equity.

I have top pair (Aces) with the nut flush draw on a board of 4h, Kh, 6s, Ad. The pot is 1800 and the bet is 600. Raising here is likely too aggressive as it could force a fold from bluffs I want to keep in or isolate me against a better hand like a set or two pair. Calling is the most profitable play, as it allows me to realize my equity with the nut flush draw while keeping the pot manageable, and allows me to re-evaluate on the river.

I've hit my flush on the river with the Queen of Hearts completing my draw. The pot is 2400 and my opponent has called both the flop and turn, suggesting they might have a strong made hand like an Ace, a King, or two pair, or perhaps a straight with JT. While any higher heart (Ace, Jack, or Ten) beats my 9-high flush, I still have a very strong hand that warrants a value bet. A half-pot bet of 1200 is an excellent size to extract value from their bluff-catchers or strong one-pair hands without over-committing against a potentially higher flush.

I have a pair of Aces (Ah, Ad) on a board of 4h, Kh, 6s, Ad, Qh. There is no flush possible for me (only four hearts present). My opponent (Pro) has been betting aggressively throughout the hand, indicating a strong range such as Top Pair with a good kicker, Two Pair, or a Set. However, I have Top Pair myself, and given the pot odds (getting 3:1 on a 1200 bet into a 3600 total pot), folding would be too tight. While I am likely losing to value hands, my hand is strong enough to call as a bluff catcher, given that the Pro could potentially be betting a worse hand or turning a missed draw into a bluff.

7-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite having the button advantage and a large chip lead, this hand has virtually no playability post-flop and extremely low equity against any reasonable calling range. It is a mandatory fold.

I am in the small blind with pocket aces, the strongest possible starting hand. With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. Raising is the optimal strategy to build the pot and maximize value. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the big blind while encouraging a call or a loose re-raise.

I am in the big blind with a weak hand, 7d 3c. Flashlight raised from the small blind to 300, which is a 3x open. Although Flashlight's stack is shorter (5775), 7-3 offsuit is at the bottom of the range and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against a range that likely dominates it. To conserve chips and wait for a better spot in the final stage of the tournament, I will fold.
I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand for this late position. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size to take the initiative and thin the field.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine of clubs, which is a strong hand for three-handed play. The button (Flashlight) has opened for a standard raise of 300. While I could 3-bet to put pressure on the button and potentially force the Big Blind (Pro) to fold, calling is a solid option that allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good post-flop playability and nut potential (flush/straight draws). I am out of position, so keeping the pot manageable is a reasonable strategy unless I want to commit to a larger 3-bet. I will call the 250 difference to see the flop.

I hold AQo in the Big Blind. The Button raised to 300 and the Small Blind called. This is a perfect squeeze opportunity. AQo is highly likely to be ahead of both the Button's wide opening range and the Small Blind's capped calling range. A 3-bet will often win the pot right here, and if called, it helps to mitigate my positional disadvantage by taking the betting lead and defining my opponents' ranges. I'll size my squeeze to 1200 to give them poor odds to call with speculative hands.

I am in position on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand. Pro has 3-bet from the big blind to 1200. While AJo can be dominated by AK or AQ, the pot odds are attractive to call and see a flop in position. Folding would be too conservative given my previous investment of 300, and 4-betting with AJo against a 3-bet from Pro is high variance. Calling is the most balanced approach to realize equity.

I initially called the 300 opening raise from the button, but the Big Blind (Pro) has since squeezed to 1200 and the button (Flashlight) called. I am now getting 3:1 pot odds (900 to call into a 2700 pot) with a suited King. Pro has shown a high frequency of aggressive 3-bets with marginal hands in earlier rounds, and Flashlight's call likely caps their range. Given the excellent price and the playability of Kc9c, I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate my position based on how the board connects.

I have a King-high flush draw on a Jack-high board in a three-bet pot. Since Pro was the pre-flop aggressor who squeezed, the most standard and effective play is to check to the raiser. This allows me to see how Pro and Flashlight react to the board. If Pro bets, I can evaluate the price to continue with my draw; if it goes check-check, I get a free card to try and hit my flush on the turn. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor would likely represent a narrow range and could force me into a difficult spot if I'm raised.

I missed the flop entirely, holding only Ace-high on a board with a flush draw and a Jack. Being out of position against two opponents who both called a significant pre-flop 3-bet, their ranges are quite strong and likely contain mid-to-high pocket pairs, broadways that hit the Jack, or club flush draws. A continuation bet into two players with just A-high is overly risky, especially considering the low stack-to-pot ratio with the Button. Checking is the safest option, aiming for pot control and potentially seeing a free turn to hit an Ace or Queen.

The board of [Jd, 2c, 5c] is relatively dry. I have top pair (Jacks) with an Ace kicker, which is a strong holding. The 3-bettor (Pro) checked to me, which often signals either a continuation of a trap with an overpair or that they missed with AK/AQ. Checking back in position allows me to control the pot size, realize equity, and re-evaluate the strength of my hand on the turn without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a range that might still have me beat or is looking for a check-raise.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds appears, which is an overcard to the Jack and connects with many broadway ranges. After the flop checked around, nobody has shown significant strength. I still have a King-high flush draw with 9 outs. Leading out now into two opponents in a bloated 3-bet pot is risky, as I could be raised and forced off my equity. By checking, I can potentially see a free river if it checks around again or evaluate the price to call if one of my opponents bets. My goal is to realize my equity and hit my flush on the river.

The turn brings the Queen of Diamonds, giving me top pair, top kicker. Since the flop checked through, my opponents' ranges are relatively capped, and my hand is likely best. However, the board is draw-heavy with both club and diamond flush draws, as well as straight draws available. I need to bet for value and protection to charge those draws and extract value from worse pairs like a Jack or a weaker Queen. A bet of around half the pot sets up a river shove against Flashlight's remaining stack.

The board is relatively dry, and while the Queen of Diamonds introduces some potential for pairs, my top pair of Jacks with an Ace kicker remains a strong holding. Pro's turn bet into a checked flop is standard but not necessarily indicative of a set; calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the river while keeping the pot manageable, whereas raising might bloat the pot against a superior range.

On the turn, Pro has led out for 1,500 and Flashlight has called. The pot is now 6,600, and it costs me 1,500 to call. My pot odds are approximately 18.5% (1500/8100). I have a King-high flush draw (9 outs), which gives me roughly a 20.5% chance to hit on the river. Since my equity is higher than the pot odds required, and I have potential implied odds if I hit against Pro's likely range of top pair or better (and Flashlight's capped range), a call is mathematically profitable. I will call to see if I can complete my flush on the river.

On the river, the board pairs the deuce, which is unlikely to have improved either opponent's hand given the pre-flop 3-bet action. My King-high has virtually no showdown value in a three-way pot where both opponents called a turn bet. However, leading out as a bluff into two opponents who have both shown interest in the pot (Pro by betting the turn and Flashlight by calling) is extremely risky and unlikely to succeed. Most pairs (Queens, Jacks, or even pocket pairs like Tens or Nines) will likely call a river bet given the pot size. I will check and hope the hand goes to a cheap showdown or fold if there is significant betting action behind me.

The river is a blank deuce, which pairs the board but misses both the club and diamond flush draws, as well as any straight draws. I hold top pair, top kicker (AQ). The pot is 8100, and my opponents have remaining stacks of 3475 and 6525, making the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) less than 1. Against their capped turn-calling ranges, my AQ is very strong and ahead of hands like KQ, QT, or a stubborn Jack. Checking would miss crucial value and let marginal hands check behind. Given the low SPR, shoving for 6525 to put both opponents all-in is the best play to extract maximum value from worse top pairs and second pairs.

The pot is 14625 and the cost to call is 3475, which provides roughly 4:1 pot odds. My top pair of Jacks (Js, Jd) with an Ace kicker acts as a significant bluff-catcher. Given the board texture [Jd, 2c, 5c, Qd, 2h], the only hands that beat me that Pro would realistically value bet are Queens, a pair of 2s, or better pocket pairs (like QQ). However, Pro's betting line could also represent an overplayed AK or a bluff, and at these pot odds, I am priced in to make the call.

I am holding King-high after missing my flush draw on the river. The action on the river is extremely heavy: the Big Blind (Pro) has shoved all-in for 6,525, and the Button (Flashlight) has called all-in for their remaining 3,475. In a three-way pot, especially after Flashlight called the shove, my King-high has absolutely no showdown value. Flashlight's call signifies they have a made hand, likely at least a pair of Queens or Jacks. Since I cannot win this hand at showdown and cannot bluff two all-in players, the only logical move is to fold.

In a heads-up situation, King-high hands are significantly stronger than in a full-ring game. With a stack of approximately 65 big blinds, I need to remain aggressive from the button to avoid being blinded down by the chip leader. K2o is a standard opening hand in this position. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position if called.

We are heads-up and I have A6o in the Big Blind. Facing a 3x open from the Button, A6o has plenty of equity against a standard Button raising range. Calling is a solid choice to see a flop without bloating the pot out of position.

On a dry Ac Td 3s flop, I hit top pair with a weak kicker. As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard and most balanced play. It allows Flash to c-bet with bluffs and weaker hands, while pot controlling against stronger Aces or better hands.

On an Ace-high board like Ac-Td-3s, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. Pro's check allows me to continue the initiative with a continuation bet. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard sizing that puts pressure on hands like middle pairs, small pairs, or gutshot draws that Pro might have defended with from the Big Blind. While I only have King-high, this is a board I should be betting frequently to represent the Ace.

Facing a half-pot continuation bet on a dry Ac Td 3s board, our top pair with a weak kicker is well ahead of Flash's c-betting range, which includes many bluffs and weaker made hands. Raising would only isolate us against better Aces (like AQ, AJ, AT) or two pairs, while folding is far too nitty. Calling is the clear standard play here to keep his bluffs in the pot and evaluate the turn.

On the turn, the standard play after calling a flop continuation bet out of position is to check again to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) with a weak top pair would mostly fold out Flash's bluffs while getting called or raised by better Aces, two pairs, or strong draws. Having the Ace of Diamonds also blocks some of his potential nut flush draws. I'll check to control the pot and evaluate his turn action.

The 8d turn is a relatively safe card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Having been called on the flop, it is likely that Pro has at least a Ten or an Ace.
